articles.title: Gaussian Point Splatting
articles.summary: Gaussian point splatting is a novel method to render large-scale scenes with many Gaussians, achieved through sampling pixel-sized points from the Gaussians and splattering them to a framebuffer using 64-bit atomics. This approach enables an even distribution of workload across millions of threads, allowing for hundreds of millions of Gaussians to be rendered in real time. By employing hierarchical frustum and occlusion culling, Gaussian point splatting can handle scenes with many different types of objects and lighting conditions.

articles.title: The C++ Standard Library Has Been Walking Itself Back for Fifteen Years, and the Receipts Are Public
articles.summary: The C++ standard library has been quietly updating itself over the past 15 years with minimal fanfare. The changes are largely driven by the need to address long-standing issues and improve performance. As a result, developers can expect significant updates to various libraries, including containers, algorithms, and strings. These updates will likely have a direct impact on the development of new applications and systems that rely heavily on C++ standard library functionality.

articles.title: How TypeScript infers type variables
articles.summary: TypeScript infers type variables defined in functions via inference, which can sometimes do what you expect or want but may not always be obvious. The process involves pairing source types with target types and then running a two-phase process to collapse candidate lists into inferred types. This is followed by applying the regular type checking algorithm to finalize the inference.

The key technology at play here is the Haskell type system, which allows developers to extend the language with new functionality while maintaining its integrity. Understanding the differences between valid and invalid extensions is crucial for developers who want to write reliable code that adheres to the language's standards. By knowing which extensions are legitimate and which impostors to avoid, developers can ensure their applications run smoothly and securely.

articles.title: I built a vulnerable app and spent $1,500 seeing if LLMs could hack it
articles.summary: Thoughts on June 3, 2026, I built a vulnerable app using React Native and Python to test whether Large Language Models (LLMs) could exploit it for hacking purposes. The goal was to see if LLMs like Google's GPT-5.5 or OpenAI's pi could reproduce common exploits found in other apps. Spending $1,500 on this experiment allowed me to run 10 runs of each target model and test their performance. My results showed that most models struggled to solve the task within a two-hour time limit, with some achieving only 6-7% success rates.
